detailed_analysis: The SharedKeyedMapLayout subsystem is small (two production files, ~430 lines) but carries several structural and correctness defects. The most serious defect is the race-detection contract in apply_slot. The docstring promises atomic semantics and 'never partially overwritten', but the implementation only re-reads the file before writing — it does not hold a lock and atomic_write_text does not perform a compare-and-swap on the inode. Two concurrent writers can both observe identical before_text and latest_text, both decide they 'won', and the slower os.replace overwrites the faster one's slot. The second correctness defect is that read_slots and apply_slot diverge in how they materialise slot text. read_slots silently injects layout.key_field into the slot mapping if the inline value omits it, but apply_slot does not strip key_field back out when writing — so a round-trip read → render → write will leave the redundant key_field permanently embedded. There is no test that exercises a shared file where the inline value already has a different value for key_field than the slot key. The third defect is in _toml_deserialize: the function tries json.loads FIRST and only falls back to tomllib.loads on JSONDecodeError. A TOML file whose entire content happens to be a JSON-parseable subset will be parsed by the JSON path instead of the TOML path. The fourth defect is global mutable state in shared_keyed_map_formats.py: _FORMAT_REGISTRY is a module-level mutable dict. Structural smells: shared_keyed_map_formats.py mixes three responsibilities — registry plumbing, JSON handling, and a hand-rolled TOML emitter plus a hand-rolled JSONC tokeniser. _strip_jsonc_comments and _strip_trailing_json_commas are each ~40 lines with nesting depth 3-4 and could be one bug away from corrupting a string literal that contains // or /* inside quotes. Test coverage gaps: no concurrent writers test, no atomic-rename failure test, no inline-slot conflict test, no non-dict slot test, no removal-of-last-slot test, no duplicate slot keys across discovery, no JSONC test, no TOML format handler test, archive test masks the key_field injection drift.

findings.problem: The module docstring promises 'The shared file is never partially overwritten' and 'detect-and-retry' against concurrent writers. The implementation only compares text snapshots before the os.replace; nothing prevents another process from writing AFTER the latest_text==before_text check but BEFORE atomic_write_text completes. os.replace is atomic at the inode level but does NOT compare-and-swap on file contents. Two writers can both observe a stable before_text, both decide they 'won', and the second os.replace silently clobbers the first. A real lock (fcntl.flock, portalocker, or O_EXCL temp + cmpxchg via stat) is required.

findings.variant: Read/write asymmetry — key_field injected on read, never stripped on write
findings.problem: read_slots injects layout.key_field into the slot mapping if absent. apply_slot writes new_slot_text verbatim without stripping the redundant key_field. Net effect: on the first round trip, a shared file that previously had {'github': {'command': ...}} becomes {'github': {'name': 'github', 'command': ...}}. This drifts user data on disk. Also: if the inline value already contains a key_field DIFFERENT from the map key, read_slots silently treats the inline value as authoritative while discovery treats the MAP key as the slot identifier.

The TOML deserialiser tries json.loads first and falls back to tomllib.loads. A file declared as TOML but happening to parse as JSON will be parsed by the JSON path, producing semantics that differ from what tomllib would have produced. There is also no test that exercises _toml_deserialize at all.

Module-level mutable dict that any caller may mutate via register_format. Registering JSON and TOML handlers executes at module import. test_register_format_is_idempotent_under_replacement signals awareness by manually restoring the prior handler — any future test that forgets that ritual will leak mutated handlers across tests.

findings.variant: Hand-rolled JSONC tokeniser, deep nesting, no edge-case coverage
findings.problem: 57-line manual state machine with 4-level nesting and cyclomatic complexity around 14. No test covers (a) // appearing inside a quoted string, (b) /* */ inside a quoted string, (c) escape sequences before " that should NOT close the string, (d) trailing comma inside a string literal followed by ].

findings.variant: Type-dispatched mega-function, no Mapping value support documented
findings.problem: Date/datetime, bytes, and None are not handled and will raise TypeError with a generic message. None in particular is plausible (a JSON null coming through canonical). Also: list-of-mappings goes through _toml_inline_table while top-level mapping goes through _emit_toml_table — no test confirms these two paths agree on round-trip.

If the shared file already has a non-dict value at map_key_path (e.g. user wrote 'mcpServers': 'disabled'), _navigate_or_create silently overwrites that value with an empty dict and proceeds to write. This is silent destruction of user data.

findings.variant: prior_value of None ambiguous (absent vs. JSON null)
findings.problem: node.get(slot_key) returns None for both 'slot absent' and 'slot present with value None'. The downstream prior_text is then None in both cases, indistinguishable to the caller.

findings.variant: Happy-path-only assertions miss key_field injection drift
findings.problem: The test does NOT compare the archived bytes to the user's original on-disk slot bytes. Because read_slots injects key_field on read (CQ-02), the archived text contains a 'name' field that the user never wrote. The assertion 'assert name in archived_obj' actually masks the bug.
There is no test for removing the LAST slot under map_key_path. Does the engine prune the empty subtree? Leave it as {}? Both observable to the user.

findings.variant: No concurrent-writer test, no atomic-rename-failure test, no JSONC test
findings.problem: The IO module's docstring asserts a concrete race contract. No test simulates a concurrent writer. No test asserts SharedKeyedMapRaceError is reachable. JSONC-tolerance branch is untested.

findings.variant: Shotgun config dict copied across two test files
findings.problem: Four helper functions and a 19-key baseline config dict are duplicated verbatim across two test files.

findings.variant: No conflict test for the inverse case
findings.problem: Asserts that two distinct (file, slot) pairs don't collide but never asserts that two identical (file, slot) pairs DO collide.
apply_slot at 56 lines exceeds the 40-line guideline and conflates four concerns: merge-into-tree, prior-value capture, race-detection, atomic write.

verdict_summary: Race contract is documented but not enforced (TOCTOU in apply_slot); read/write asymmetry on key_field silently mutates user data on disk; TOML deserialiser dispatch is wrong; tests skew to happy path with no concurrency, no atomic-failure, no last-slot-removal, no inline-key conflict, and no JSONC coverage.
